transcript:
:
In my experience, Metformin is the single most misunderstood drug in modern medicine. So, we're talking about a medication that millions of people take every single day for type 2 diabetes, but increasingly it's being swallowed by healthy individuals in their 30s, 40s and 50s who are trying to slow down aging or lose weight or improve their metabolic health without actually having a diabetes diagnosis. And what I'm going to do in this video today is walk you through the things that almost nobody tells you about this drug. The mechanisms that actually explain why it might be working against your fitness goals, the nutrient deficiencies that can creep up on you after years of use, and the very specific situations where this drug is genuinely lifesaving versus where it might actually be shortening your healthspan. And then at the end, I'll give you the exact framework that I use with my own patients to decide who should actually be taking this and what you should be doing instead if you're one of the healthy people just looking to optimize your longevity. If you're new to the channel, then hello. I'm Dr. Alex. I'm an emergency medicine doctor with an obsession about the things that make us live longer and what truly prevents the diseases that we see every day in the emergency department. Now, I've been looking at the Metformin research pretty closely over the last few years because the number of otherwise healthy patients coming into A&E who are taking this drug has absolutely skyrocketed, and very often they're experiencing side effects that they were never warned about. And what you'll notice here is that my approach to this drug is probably quite different from what you've heard on other channels or from longevity influencers who seem to suggest that everyone over the age of 40 should be popping this pill regardless of their health status. So, the first thing that we need to understand is what Metformin actually is and why it became so popular outside of diabetes treatment. So, Metformin is a biguanide medication that has been used since the 1950s primarily to lower blood glucose in type 2 diabetes. It works mainly by reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis, which is essentially the process by which your liver makes new glucose and releases it into your bloodstream, and it also improves insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues like your muscles. Now, over the last decade or so, it has gained this almost mythical status in the longevity community because of something called the TAME trial, which stands for Targeting Aging with Metformin. This was proposed as the first major trial to test whether a drug could actually slow down aging in humans who don't have diabetes. And the theory was based on observational studies suggesting that diabetic patients taking Metformin seemed to live longer than non-diabetic controls, and that it might reduce cancer risk and cardiovascular disease beyond just controlling blood sugar. But stay with me because this is where the narrative starts to diverge from the biology. Now, the reason that Metformin is being promoted as an anti-aging drug is that it activates an enzyme called AMPK, which is essentially your cellular energy sensor. When AMPK is activated, it triggers a cascade of metabolic changes that look beneficial on paper: increased fat oxidation, improved insulin sensitivity, reduced inflammation, and activation of autophagy, which is your cellular recycling process. And all of these things are associated with longevity in animal models. So, on the surface, taking Metformin seems like a way to get the benefits of calorie restriction or exercise in a pill form. But here's the biology behind this point that changes everything. So, Metformin doesn't just gently nudge your metabolism toward health. It accumulates in your mitochondria, which are the power plants of your cells, and there it inhibits Complex I of the electron transport chain. Now, this is absolutely critical to understand, so let me break this down. Your mitochondria generate ATP, which is cellular energy, by passing electrons through a series of protein complexes. Complex I is the entry point for this process. When Metformin inhibits Complex I, it essentially creates a partial block in your energy production line. Your cells sense this energy stress, and that's what triggers the AMPK activation. So, the longevity benefits aren't coming from Metformin being a nutrient or a helpful supplement. They're coming from your cells reacting to a mild mitochondrial toxin. You're essentially inducing a low-grade energy crisis that forces your cells into survival mode. Now, the flip side is really, really telling here. So, if you're someone who exercises regularly, and I mean really exercises with intensity, then this mitochondrial inhibition isn't just a theoretical concern. It's actively working against one of the most powerful longevity interventions we have. And the scale of this is really quite interesting. So, there was a landmark study published in Aging Cell in 2021 that looked at healthy older adults taking Metformin versus placebo while undergoing supervised aerobic training. And what they found was that the Metformin group had significantly blunted improvements in mitochondrial respiration and VO2 max compared to the placebo group. Let me just say that again. The people taking the supposedly longevity-enhancing drug got less benefit from their exercise in terms of cardiovascular fitness and mitochondrial function. That statement is staggering when you consider that VO2 max is one of the strongest predictors of all-cause mortality that we have. Another study from the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism found that Metformin attenuated the increases in skeletal muscle mitochondrial complex IV activity that normally occur with exercise training. So, essentially, if you're taking Metformin and training hard, you're leaving gains on the table, and these aren't just aesthetic gains or muscle size. We're talking about the fundamental cellular machinery that keeps you alive and energetic as you age. Moving on to point number two then, which is the B12 deficiency issue. Now, this isn't just a minor side effect that you can ignore. In my clinical practice, I've seen patients who have been on Metformin for five or ten years who come in with neurological symptoms, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive fog, and macrocytic anemia, and when we check their B12 levels, they're in the basement. And the really concerning thing is that this develops so gradually that most people don't notice until there's actual nerve damage. So, the mechanism here is that Metformin interferes with calcium-dependent absorption of vitamin B12 in your terminal ileum. It essentially binds to the B12-intrinsic factor complex and prevents its uptake. Now, the research on this is quite robust. There was a systematic review and meta-analysis published in the journal Diabetes Care that pooled data from multiple studies and found that Metformin use was associated with a roughly 2.5-fold increased risk of vitamin B12 deficiency, and that the risk increases with both dose and duration. After ten years of use, some studies suggest that up to 30% of patients have significantly depleted B12 stores. And here's why this matters for longevity specifically. Vitamin B12 is absolutely essential for methylation, which is a biochemical process that regulates everything from DNA repair to neurotransmitter synthesis to homocysteine metabolism. Elevated homocysteine, which results from B12 deficiency, is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke, and dementia. So, while you're taking Metformin hoping to prevent heart disease and cognitive decline, you might actually be accelerating both if you're not meticulously monitoring your B12 status. And I would say this is probably one of the most important things in this video today. If you are going to take Metformin, you need to be checking your B12 levels annually at absolute minimum, and preferably every six months, and you need to be taking a high-quality methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin supplement, not just cyanocobalamin, because the calcium-dependent block means you need much higher doses to overcome the malabsorption. Okay, so moving on to the issue that keeps me busiest in the emergency department, and that's lactic acidosis. Now, Metformin-associated lactic acidosis, or MALA, is rare but it is potentially fatal, and it represents one of the most serious complications of this drug. The mechanism goes back to that Complex I inhibition in your mitochondria. When you inhibit mitochondrial respiration, your cells switch to anaerobic glycolysis for energy, which produces lactate as a byproduct. Under normal circumstances, your liver clears this lactate, but if you have any impairment in renal function, or if you become severely dehydrated, or if you have a serious infection or sepsis, the Metformin accumulates and the lactate builds up to dangerous levels. Now, the absolute risk is actually quite low in healthy individuals with normal kidney function. We're talking about roughly 3 to 9 cases per 100,000 patient-years of use. But the risk increases dramatically if your estimated glomerular filtration rate, or eGFR, drops below 45, and Metformin is absolutely contraindicated below an eGFR of 30. The tragedy is that kidney function declines with age naturally, and many people in their 60s and 70s who have been stable on Metformin for years don't realize that their renal function has quietly deteriorated until they end up in A&E with a pH of 7.1 and lactate levels through the roof. And in the emergency department, MALA is one of the most terrifying presentations because the treatment is essentially supportive care while we wait for the kidneys to clear the drug, and in severe cases, patients need dialysis. The mortality rate for severe lactic acidosis can be as high as 50%. So, if you're taking Metformin and you haven't had your kidney function checked in the last six months, you need to book a blood test tomorrow. This isn't something you can guess at based on how you feel. Now, let's talk about who actually benefits from this drug, because this is where I want to be really clear and hopefully helpful. If you have type 2 diabetes, or perhaps prediabetes with significant insulin resistance and elevated fasting glucose, then Metformin is one of the safest and most effective medications we have. The UK Prospective Diabetes Study, or UKPDS, showed that Metformin reduces diabetes-related endpoints and all-cause mortality in overweight diabetic patients compared to diet alone or other treatments. For diabetics, the benefits clearly outweigh the risks, provided you monitor kidney function and B12. However, if you are a healthy individual with normal glucose tolerance, normal HbA1c, and you're taking Metformin because you heard it might help you live longer, the risk-benefit calculation changes completely. You're exposing yourself to B12 deficiency, potential exercise interference, and the small but real risk of lactic acidosis, all for benefits that have never actually been proven in randomized controlled trials in non-diabetic populations. The TAME trial is still ongoing, and until we have those results, prescribing Metformin for longevity in healthy people is essentially an experiment based on theoretical mechanisms and observational data that may not translate. The observational studies showing longevity benefits had major confounders, diabetic patients taking Metformin were being compared to diabetic patients not taking it, or to the general population, but compliant medication taking is itself a marker of health consciousness and healthcare access. So, what should you be doing instead if you're looking for the metabolic benefits without the side effects? Well, the first and most obvious answer is exercise, but specifically the type that activates AMPK naturally without poisoning your mitochondria. Zone 2 cardiovascular training, which I've talked about extensively in other videos, creates the exact same AMPK activation and mitochondrial biogenesis that Metformin mimics, but it actually improves mitochondrial function rather than inhibiting it. A 30-minute brisk walk after meals, which I do every morning with Mango, improves insulin sensitivity and glucose uptake through GLUT4 translocation in your muscles, and it does this without blocking Complex I. The research on this is absolutely solid. There was a study in JAMA Internal Medicine following over 80,000 adults that found brisk walking for 75 minutes per week was associated with a 24% lower risk of premature death. That's a real intervention with real data, not theoretical longevity benefits. The second alternative is time-restricted eating or intermittent fasting. When you fast for 14 to 16 hours, you drop your insulin levels and activate AMPK and autophagy naturally. This is essentially free, has no side effects, and creates the same cellular stress responses that Metformin induces, but it does so while upregulating mitochondrial biogenesis through PGC-1alpha, not downregulating it. I personally eat my evening meal early and delay breakfast until 10 or 11am when I'm not working shifts, and this gives me a 16-hour overnight fast that costs nothing and requires no prescription. The third alternative is berberine, which is a natural compound found in several plants that also activates AMPK and has been shown in studies to lower blood glucose comparable to Metformin in some diabetic populations. Now, berberine also inhibits mitochondrial function similarly to Metformin, so it has some of the same concerns regarding exercise adaptation, but for people who can't tolerate Metformin's gastrointestinal side effects, it can be an option. However, I would argue that if you're going to use a mitochondrial inhibitor, you should at least know that you're doing so and not pretend it's a harmless supplement. Alpha-lipoic acid is another option that improves insulin sensitivity through different mechanisms and actually supports mitochondrial function as an antioxidant. Now, let me just make a really specific point here because I know some people will be expecting me to suggest a specific dosing protocol or tell you how to source Metformin online if your doctor won't prescribe it. I am not going to do that. I have been offered literally tens of thousands of pounds to promote various longevity supplements and off-label medication strategies on this channel, and there's a reason why I've declined every single one of them. The populations around the world who live the longest, the Blue Zones, do not take Metformin. What they do is they eat real food, they move regularly throughout the day, they have strong social connections, and they have a sense of purpose. They don't rely on pills to offset a sedentary lifestyle or a diet high in processed foods. There was a huge study that pooled data from nearly 300 randomized controlled trials looking at supplements in the general population, and it concluded that most showed no significant benefit for cardiovascular outcomes or mortality, and in some cases caused harm. So, do not spend your money or risk your health on unproven longevity drugs when the interventions that actually work are completely free and available to everyone. Right, then let me pull all of this together and tell you what my actual framework is for deciding whether someone should take Metformin. So, first, I look at their HbA1c and fasting glucose. If they're diabetic or clearly prediabetic with an HbA1c over 6.5%, then Metformin is on the table. Second, I check kidney function meticulously. If eGFR is above 45 and stable, we can proceed. If it's borderline, we look for alternatives. Third, I assess their exercise capacity and goals. If they are an athlete or someone training for longevity with high intensity sessions, I am very hesitant to prescribe Metformin because of the mitochondrial interference. I would rather optimize their training and nutrition first. Fourth, I check B12 status baseline and plan for annual monitoring with supplementation. Fifth, I review other medications. If they're taking proton pump inhibitors or H2 blockers for acid reflux, the B12 absorption issue becomes even worse, so we need to be extra careful or avoid Metformin entirely. And sixth, I discuss the lactic acidosis risk and ensure they understand to stop the medication immediately if they get severely ill, dehydrated, or are scheduled for contrast imaging studies that might stress the kidneys. If all of these criteria are met and the patient understands the risks, then Metformin can be a valuable tool. But if you're a healthy 40-year-old with normal glucose and good fitness, taking Metformin is like putting a restrictor plate on a race car engine. You might think you're preventing wear and tear, but you're actually limiting the peak performance that drives the health benefits you're seeking. The whole point of this channel is that prevention is consistently easier than treatment, but that doesn't mean prevention requires medication. True prevention means building metabolic resilience through sleep, movement, nutrition, and stress management. It means maintaining muscle mass through resistance training, which by the way is one of the strongest predictors of longevity and is actively undermined by mitochondrial dysfunction. It means keeping your kidneys healthy through hydration and avoiding nephrotoxic drugs when possible. And it means getting sufficient B12 through diet and absorption support. Now, a lot of you may be wondering what I personally do, and that is a fair question. I do not take Metformin. My fasting glucose runs about 4.8 to 5.0, my HbA1c is in the low 5s, and I achieve my AMPK activation through the morning routine I outlined in my previous video: early light exposure, cold exposure, fasted exercise, and time-restricted eating. I track my heart rate variability with my Garmin, I monitor my blood pressure weekly, and I focus on the fundamentals that actually move the needle. If my metabolic health changes as I age, I will reconsider, but for now, the risk-benefit analysis clearly favors lifestyle over pharmaceuticals. So, to summarize everything we've covered today: Metformin inhibits mitochondrial Complex I, which triggers AMPK activation but potentially blunts exercise adaptations. It causes B12 deficiency in up to 30% of long-term users, which can lead to irreversible neurological damage. It carries a small but serious risk of lactic acidosis if kidney function declines. It is potentially appropriate for diabetics and some prediabetics under medical supervision, but likely inappropriate for healthy individuals seeking longevity. And the alternatives, exercise, fasting, and dietary optimization, provide similar metabolic benefits without the mitochondrial toxicity. The common theme running through all of these videos on this channel is that small things done consistently and improved over time is where the real health gains come from. Taking a pill might feel like you're being proactive, but if that pill interferes with your ability to exercise effectively, maintain neurological health, or puts you at risk of acidosis, then it's not longevity medicine. It's just another way of medicalizing a lifestyle problem. Focus on the basics. Get your morning light. Do your zone 2 cardio. Lift weights. Eat protein and fiber. Sleep consistently. And monitor your actual health metrics rather than assuming a drug is protecting you. That's how you actually live longer and better. If you've found this video useful, then please do consider subscribing because I've got loads more content coming on metabolic health, cardiovascular disease prevention, and what actually works based on the evidence rather than the hype. And as always, if you are currently taking Metformin, do not stop it without speaking to your doctor, especially if you have diabetes. But do ask them about B12 testing, kidney function monitoring, and whether the exercise interference data might apply to your specific situation. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.
topic: Metformin Side Effects, Exercise Interference, and Longevity Risks
title: Taking Metformin? You Were Never Told This...
